changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Premonition of Rainy Days"

TextENGNew: I opened my favorite window, laid a cushion there as I always do, and settled in to enjoy my modest little verandah.
changeReason: Old omits several details from the Japanese: お気に入りの (favorite) window, いつものように (as always), and ささやかな縁側 (modest verandah), which establish routine and setting.

TextENGNew: ...And yet, here comes one noisy creature stomping about and ruining my precious quiet evening.
changeReason: Old omits the animal counter 一匹 and onomatopoeia どたどた (stomping); the Japanese deliberately uses an animal counter to describe the person, which the new translation preserves with 'one noisy creature.'

TextENGNew: Thanks to her, my cool and pleasant evening hour is utterly ruined.
"...You look happy. Ah, right — you got to eat lots of your favorite sweets today, didn't you."
This child simply cannot resist anything sweet.
...I don't dislike sweets myself, but eating that much would give me heartburn.
For the moment, since she looks so happy, I'll leave her be.
For a while now I've been feeding her nothing but spicy and salty things to my own taste, so she must have been overjoyed.
Of course, for me too it was a truly enjoyable day.
It was enough for me to sit here savoring that joy while the evening cool settled in around me.
changeReason: Old translates 夕涼み as 'enjoying the sunset' but it actually means 'enjoying the evening cool/breeze'; new correctly captures this.

TextENGNew: But when I looked up at the sky, I could see clouds that carried just a hint of weight.
changeReason: Old says 'thick clouds' but the Japanese ほんの少しだけ重みのある雲 describes clouds with only a slight heaviness; new preserves this subtlety.

TextENGNew: ...The wind, too, feels just a touch too cool.
It may turn into an evening shower.
changeReason: Old uses generic 'raining' but the Japanese 夕立 specifically means an evening/afternoon shower, which the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: "...There are stories that deep-sea fish — ones you would never ordinarily see in the shallows — sometimes appear there as a warning of great earthquakes or tsunamis."
changeReason: Old says 'deep-sea animals' but Japanese says 深海の魚 (deep-sea fish); also omits the emphasis that these fish would 'never normally be seen' in the shallows.

TextENGNew: Today, in our shallows, we saw a rare deep-sea fish that should never appear there.
As with most legends of sea monsters, people despise deep-sea creatures and are quick to see them as harbingers of ill fortune.
Whether it appears alive, or washes up as a corpse.
Ah — that's a rather apt way of putting it. Rather interesting, in fact.
Whether it appears alive, or washes up as a corpse. An omen of misfortune — is that it.
changeReason: Old misses the questioning/pondering tone of か at the end and renders it as a declarative statement; new preserves this reflective quality.

TextENGNew: Ah... it may rain after all.
"...I know, you don't have to say it. I'll bring in the laundry."
I seized the laundry basket and headed outside to take in the washing fluttering on the clothesline by the front of the house.
The clouds were growing more and more lead-grey.
changeReason: Old uses generic 'darker' but the Japanese specifies 鉛色 (lead-grey), a specific and evocative color choice that the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: I like rain... but I had a feeling I wouldn't be able to like it today.
